# Shotoku

> Local-first authorization layer for AI agents.

Shotoku intercepts agent actions — API calls, purchases, code execution, MCP tool use — evaluates them against a local policy file, and records every decision to an append-only local ledger. No cloud dependency in the authorization path. No custody, no wallet, no payment processing.

Last updated: 2026-07-06

---

## What Shotoku is

Shotoku is an authorization layer, not a payment rail. Spending is the first use case. Authorization for any agent action is the category.

Before an agent acts, call `authorize()`. Shotoku reads your policy file and the local ledger (for rolling budget totals), evaluates the request, writes the decision, and returns a verdict with structured reasons. If the policy file is missing or the request is malformed, Shotoku denies — it never defaults to approved.

Three outcomes are possible:

- **approved** — the action passed all policy checks. The agent can proceed.
- **denied** — a policy rule blocked the action. The agent should stop.
- **pending_approval** — no rule automatically decided this. A human must approve or deny via `shotoku approve <id>`.

---

## Key concepts

### AI agent authorization

AI agent authorization is the practice of requiring an explicit policy check before an autonomous agent takes an action — spending money, calling an API, executing code, sending a message, or using a tool. Without authorization, agents act on behalf of users with no record, no limits, and no approval step.

Shotoku implements this as a local-first gate: your policy lives on your machine, evaluation happens on your machine, and decisions are stored on your machine. Nothing is sent to a remote service.

### AI agent approval workflow

When an agent requests an action that no rule automatically approves, Shotoku records it as `pending_approval` and stops. A human reviews it with `shotoku status`, approves with `shotoku approve <id>`, or denies with `shotoku deny <id>`. The decision is appended to the ledger and the agent can re-run.

### Hash-chained audit log for AI agents

Every decision Shotoku records includes an integrity block linking it to the hash of the previous entry. This makes the ledger tamper-evident: altering a past record breaks every hash that follows it. The ledger is a plain JSONL file — one JSON object per line — readable in any text editor and verifiable with `shotoku verify`.

### x402 authorization

x402 is an HTTP payment protocol (HTTP 402 Payment Required) for agent-to-service micropayments, settled in stablecoins. Shotoku authorizes the payment intent before execution: when an agent encounters a 402 response, it calls `authorize()` with `action: "purchase"` and `rail: "x402"` before signing. If denied or pending, the payment never fires.

---

## Core API

### authorize(request, options)

```typescript
import { authorize } from "@shotoku/core";

const response = await authorize(
  {
    actor:    "agent-001",
    action:   "api_call",
    resource: "api.openai.com",
    amount:   0.04,
  },
  {
    policyPath: "./policy.yaml",
    ledgerPath: "./data/decisions.jsonl",
  },
);

if (response.approved) {
  await callOpenAI(prompt);
}
```

### AuthorizeRequest

```typescript
interface AuthorizeRequest {
  readonly actor:    string;               // agent name or ID
  readonly action:   AgentAction;          // what the agent wants to do
  readonly resource: string;               // target domain or service
  readonly rail?:    ExecutionRail;        // optional: x402 | mcp | api | code | custom
  readonly amount?:  number;               // optional: USD cost of the action
  readonly context?: Record<string, unknown>; // optional: extra fields recorded with the decision
}
```

### AuthorizeResponse

```typescript
interface AuthorizeResponse {
  readonly approved:    boolean;
  readonly status:      "approved" | "denied" | "pending_approval";
  readonly reasons:     readonly ReasonItem[];
  readonly explanation: Explanation;
  readonly decisionId:  string;   // e.g. dec_abc123
  readonly timestamp:   string;   // ISO 8601
}
```

### AgentAction

```
purchase | api_call | execute_code | send_email | mcp_tool | custom
```

### ExecutionRail

```
x402 | mcp | api | code | custom
```

---

## Policy format

A policy file is a YAML file on your machine. Rules are evaluated top-to-bottom — the first match wins. Unknown fields are rejected to prevent typos from silently weakening authorization.

```yaml
version: 1
defaultVerdict: pending_approval

rules:
  - resource: "api.openai.com"
    actions: ["api_call"]
    verdict: approved
    maxAmount: 5.00
    maxDailyAmount: 50.00

  - resource: "api.anthropic.com"
    actions: ["api_call"]
    verdict: approved
    maxAmount: 2.00
    maxDailyAmount: 20.00

  - resource: "*"
    actions: ["purchase"]
    verdict: denied
```

`maxDailyAmount` is checked against a rolling 24-hour window per actor+resource pair, computed from the local ledger.

---

## CLI commands

```
shotoku init                          # scaffold policy.yaml and data/ directory
shotoku authorize --actor --action --resource [--amount]
shotoku status                        # show pending approvals
shotoku approve <decisionId>          # approve a pending decision
shotoku deny <decisionId>             # deny a pending decision
shotoku history [--actor] [--since] [--status]
shotoku decision <id>                 # show full decision detail
shotoku verify                        # verify ledger hash chain integrity
```

Exit codes: `0` if approved, `1` if denied or pending.

---

## MCP server

Shotoku exposes an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server so agent hosts — Claude, Cursor, Windsurf — can call authorization tools inline, without custom integration code.

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "shotoku": {
      "command": "node",
      "args": ["/path/to/shotoku/mcp/dist/index.js"],
      "env": {
        "SHOTOKU_POLICY_PATH": "./policy.yaml",
        "SHOTOKU_LEDGER_PATH": "./data/decisions.jsonl"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

MCP tools exposed: `authorize_action`, `get_decision`, `get_pending_approvals`.

---

## What Shotoku is not

- Not a wallet. Shotoku does not hold funds.
- Not a payment processor. It does not move money.
- Not a custody provider. It never touches private keys.
- Not a cloud service. The authorization path requires no network.
- Not an enterprise compliance suite. It is built for agent developers.

---

## Documentation

- [Quickstart](https://shotoku.dev/docs/quickstart): Install to first decision in five minutes
- [API reference](https://shotoku.dev/docs/api): authorize(), types, request/response shape
- [Policies](https://shotoku.dev/docs/policies): Policy YAML format, rules, limits, wildcards
- [CLI reference](https://shotoku.dev/docs/cli): Every command, flag, and exit code
- [MCP server](https://shotoku.dev/docs/mcp): Connect Shotoku to Claude, Cursor, and any MCP client
- [x402](https://shotoku.dev/docs/x402): Authorize HTTP 402 payment intents before they execute
- [TUI](https://shotoku.dev/docs/tui): Interactive terminal UI for reviewing pending approvals
- [Snapshots](https://shotoku.dev/docs/snapshots): Sign and verify policy and ledger state

## Source

- GitHub: https://github.com/shotoku-dev/shotoku
- npm (CLI): https://www.npmjs.com/package/shotoku-cli
- npm (core): https://www.npmjs.com/package/@shotoku/core
